does: This paper introduces EVA, a model editing framework that addresses jailbreak vulnerabilities in large language and vision-language models through targeted neuron editing rather than broad retraining. EVA identifies specific neurons responsible for models responding to adversarial triggers and surgically modifies them to reject harmful instructions. The approach reframes safety alignment as a knowledge correction problem, localizing edits to preserve model capabilities on benign tasks. By operating at the neuron level, EVA reduces computational overhead compared to safety fine-tuning while maintaining the model's general performance.
connects: This work builds on recent advances in model editing and mechanistic interpretability that enable precise modification of learned behaviors without full retraining. It extends prior safety alignment research, which has relied on broad fine-tuning methods like instruction tuning or reinforcement learning from human feedback, both of which typically incur significant computational costs and suffer from safety-utility tradeoffs. EVA also connects to growing literature on jailbreak vulnerabilities in language and vision models, responding to demonstrated weaknesses in standard defense mechanisms like external filters and post-hoc safeguards that struggle with adversarial visual or textual triggers.
enables: This neuron-level editing approach opens new directions for surgical safety interventions in large models, potentially enabling efficient defenses deployable to already-trained systems without retraining. The framework could facilitate domain-specific safety customization, where different threat models require different neuron modifications without redundant training. Future work might combine EVA with mechanistic understanding of how models encode harmful knowledge, enabling more targeted interventions. The technique also suggests possibilities for studying what makes models vulnerable to jailbreaks at a computational level, potentially informing both better attacks and defenses in an interpretability-informed safety paradigm.
